summary: Zhang Fei dies. At Langzhong preparing the Yiling vanguard. Drunk and grieving for Guan Yu, demands his officers Fan Jiang and Zhang Da produce white mourning silk for the whole army in three days; flogs them bloody when they protest impossible. The two slit Zhang Fei's throat in his sleep that night, take the head, defect to Sun Quan. The third of the oath-brothers gone - by his own cruelty as much as by the assassins' knives.
Zhang Da; one of Zhang Fei's two assassins; flees with Fan Jiang to Sun Quan with Zhang Fei's head. Will be sent back bound by Sun Quan in chapter 83 as a vain peace offering.

Liu Bei; the chapter's emotional fulcrum. Wakes from a dream of Zhang Fei before the news arrives; faints when the messenger reads the report. With Guan Yu and Zhang Fei both dead, the war against Wu is now unstoppable - he overrides Zhuge Liang and Zhao Yun's objections and marches. The Yiling campaign begins.
Guan Yu; named throughout in the recap of why Liu Bei rides east.
Zhuge Liang; argues against the expedition - Cao Pi is the greater threat; vengeance against Wu plays into Wei's hands. Liu Bei dismisses him.
Zhao Yun; argues with Zhuge Liang against the expedition; ordered to stay behind to guard the rear.
Guan Xing; rides in the Shu vanguard now in his father's place; sworn to vengeance against Pan Zhang and Ma Zhong.
Zhang Bao; rides in the Shu vanguard now in his father's place; sworn brother to Guan Xing through the campaign. The new generation.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Cao Cao; named in passing in the recap of his death.
Cao Pi; new Emperor of Wei; receives reports of Shu mobilising against Wu - moves to take advantage.
Sun Quan; receives Fan Jiang and Zhang Da bringing Zhang Fei's head; horrified at the consequences.
Lu Jing; on Sun Quan's council; counsels conciliation with Shu.
Zhuge Jin; sent again as Wu peace envoy to Liu Bei; turned away.
Chen Zhen; in Liu Bei's court.
Fa Zheng; named in passing - Zhuge Liang laments that Fa Zheng's death (off-stage between chapters) means there is no one left whose counsel Liu Bei would heed.
Huang Zhong; with the Shu vanguard; eager despite his age - will die in the next battle. The Five Tigers shrinking.
Ma Chao; ordered to defend the Hanzhong frontier against any Wei opportunism.
Ma Dai; with Ma Chao.
Ma Liang; with the Shu expedition as Liu Bei's recorder.
Huang Quan; with the Shu expedition; commands the north-bank army.
Wei Yan; with the Shu army.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Feng Xi; Shu officer with the Yiling expedition.
Feng Xi (duplicate record).
Zhang Nan; Shu officer with the Yiling expedition.
Zhang Nan (duplicate record).
Qin Mi; in Liu Bei's court; counsels against the expedition; brushed aside.
Ma Zhong; the Wu officer who captured Guan Yu; one of Guan Xing's revenge targets.
Ma Zhong (duplicate record).
Pan Zhang; the Wu officer who commanded the Linju ambush; one of Guan Xing's revenge targets.
Mi Fang; Wu turncoat who took Gong'an from Shu; one of the revenge targets.
Zhang He; named in passing on the Wei side.
Yan Yan; on the Shu side.
Yan Yan (duplicate record).
Li Yin; minor Shu officer named in passing.
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.
Zhang Ni; minor Shu officer.
Zhang Shao; minor figure named in passing.
Zhang Yi; minor figure.
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhao Rong; minor figure named in passing.

Liu Bei; advances the Yiling expedition. Sun Quan, panicked, sends ever more peace embassies; Liu Bei rejects them all. Then Sun Quan formally pledges vassalage to Cao Pi to free his northern flank - Cao Pi accepts and confers the title 'King of Wu' on Sun Quan.

Sun Quan; on the defensive - sends Zhao Zi to Cao Pi to seek vassalage; receives the title King of Wu in exchange for nominal submission. Cao Pi's gift includes nine grades of ceremonial regalia. The diplomatic move that frees Sun Quan to focus on Liu Bei.
Cao Pi; receives Sun Quan's submission; confers the King of Wu title; Liu Ye argues for striking the moment Sun Quan is committed against Shu - Cao Pi prefers to watch.
Zhao Zi; Sun Quan's envoy to Cao Pi; conducts the celebrated repartee with Cao Pi when asked if his lord is a hero - gives a clever answer that wins Cao Pi's respect and the deal.
Zhao Zi (duplicate record).
Liu Ye; argues Cao Pi should attack Wu while it is engaged against Shu - Cao Pi declines.
Jia Xu; in the Wei court.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).

Huang Zhong dies. Stings at Liu Bei's chance remark that the old generals are past it; charges alone into the Wu lines at the next engagement; mortally wounded by an arrow from Pan Zhang's officer Ma Zhong; dies at camp. The last of the Five Tigers (after Guan Yu and Zhang Fei) gone - Ma Chao and Zhao Yun remain.
Guan Xing; in the Shu vanguard.
Zhang Bao; in the Shu vanguard with Guan Xing.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Gan Ning; in the Wu army; will be killed at the river crossing by the Man chieftain Shamoke's arrow in the next chapter.
Pan Zhang; in the Wu defense; killed by Guan Xing in revenge for Guan Yu - Guan Xing recovers his father's Green Dragon saber from him.
Han Dang; senior Wu commander.
Zhou Tai; senior Wu commander.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).
Xu Sheng; senior Wu commander.
Sun Huan; young Wu kinsman; sent to the front; fails against the Shu vanguard.
Sun Huan (duplicate record).
Lu Su; named in passing - the old generation.
Zhou Yu; named in passing - the old generation.
Zhuge Jin; the Wu peace envoy again, rejected by Liu Bei.
Zhuge Liang; in Chengdu, watching.
Gu Yong; on Sun Quan's council.
Zhang Zhao; on Sun Quan's council.
Zhang Zhao (duplicate record).
Zhu Ran; with the Wu army.
Guan Yu; named throughout in the recap - the cause of war.
Cao Cao; named in passing - already dead.
Feng Xi; Shu officer in the campaign.
Feng Xi (duplicate record).
Huang Quan; with the Shu north-bank army.
Zhang Nan; Shu officer.
Zhang Nan (duplicate record).
Li Yi; Wu officer in the campaign.
Li Yi (duplicate record).
Li Yi (duplicate record).
Li Yi (duplicate record).
Li Yi (duplicate record).
Xie Jing; Wu officer.
Xie Jing (duplicate record).
Xing Zhen; Wu officer.
Liu Ning; Wu officer.
Du Lu; minor figure named in passing.
Kuai Yue; named in passing - the old generation.
Liang Da; minor figure.
Lady Sun; named in passing.
Lady Sun (duplicate record).
Sun Jian; named in passing - the founding father.
Yu Jin; named in passing - already dead of shame.

Lu Xun; the chapter's incoming master. Sun Quan appoints him supreme commander of the Wu armies on Kan Ze's recommendation - over the protests of older generals like Han Dang and Zhou Tai who consider him a young scholar. Lu Xun pulls all Wu forces back inside their stockades, refuses to engage, lets Liu Bei's army wear itself out in the summer heat strung along 700 li of forest camps. Sets up the fire-attack of next chapter.
Kan Ze; recommends Lu Xun to Sun Quan; the kingmaker for the man who will win Yiling.
Sun Quan; appoints Lu Xun supreme commander with full authority - the sword of office, the ceremony of free command.

Liu Bei; advances on the Wu camps; cannot draw Lu Xun out; reaches Yiling. Strings his 700,000-man army (the figure is novelistic) along a chain of 40 connected stockaded camps stretching seven hundred li through pine forest. The heat is brutal; he orders the camps moved into the woods for shade - the strategic mistake.
Ma Liang; tries to dissuade Liu Bei from the forested camps; carries Liu Bei's camp-map back to Zhuge Liang in Chengdu for review (Zhuge Liang will react in horror).
Ma Zhong; killed by Guan Xing in revenge - Guan Xing chases him down the riverbank.
Ma Zhong (duplicate record).
Mi Fang; one of the Jingzhou turncoats; murders Fu Shiren in his sleep; brings the head to Liu Bei seeking mercy. Liu Bei has him executed for the betrayal of Guan Yu.
Zhang Da; one of Zhang Fei's assassins. Sun Quan ships him bound back to Liu Bei as a peace token; Liu Bei has him cut to pieces at Zhang Fei's altar.
Guan Xing; runs down Ma Zhong; recovers his father's reputation.
Zhang Bao; with Guan Xing through the campaign.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).

Gan Ning dies. Killed by an arrow from the Man chieftain Shamoke at the Fuchi River crossing while ill with fever; manages to ride to a great tree and die seated upright. The last great Wu warrior of the founding generation gone.
Han Dang; old guard Wu commander; protests Lu Xun's promotion but is forced to obey.
Zhou Tai; old guard Wu commander.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).
Xu Sheng; under Lu Xun.
Sun Huan; with the Wu army; defeated by Zhang Bao early in the chapter.
Sun Huan (duplicate record).
Pan Zhang; killed in revenge by Guan Xing - Guan Yu's Green Dragon saber returns to his son.
Liu Shan; in Chengdu.
Huang Quan; with the Shu north-bank army.
Huang Zhong; named in passing in the recap of his death.
Feng Xi; Shu officer in the camps.
Feng Xi (duplicate record).
Zhang Nan; Shu officer in the camps.
Zhang Nan (duplicate record).
Zhang Fei; named in passing - the assassin Zhang Da delivered for his altar.
Guan Yu; named throughout in the war's recap.
Gu Yong; on Sun Quan's council.
Lu Jing; on Sun Quan's council.
Lu Jun; with Lu Xun's staff.
Lu Su; named in passing - the old generation.
Lu Yi; minor figure named in passing.
Lu Yu; minor figure named in passing.
Lu Yu (duplicate record).
Shi Ji; minor figure named in passing.
Lady Sun; named in passing - when she hears of the war's casualties she throws herself into the river.
Lady Sun (duplicate record).
Sun Ce; named in passing - Sun Quan's elder brother, founder of Wu.
Zhang Zhao; on Sun Quan's council.
Zhang Zhao (duplicate record).
Zhou Yu; named in passing - the old generation.
Bu Zhi; on Sun Quan's council.
Cheng Bing; minor Wu officer named in passing.
Ding Feng; in Lu Xun's army.
Ding Feng (duplicate record).
Liang Da; minor figure.

Lu Xun; springs the fire-attack at Yiling. With his stockaded camps in summer drought, his men carry sulphur and saltpetre on their backs in concealed bundles; in a single coordinated night every Shu camp along the seven hundred li is torched. Forty contiguous Shu stockades go up at once; the chained fire roars through the pine forests. Liu Bei's army is annihilated. Lu Xun pursues but is stopped at the Yufu Gorge by Zhuge Liang's pre-set Eight Trigrams stone maze - the famous 'stone array' that holds the pursuer fast until Zhuge Liang's father-in-law Huang Chengyan leads him out.

Liu Bei; the catastrophe. Wakes in his command tent to find the night red with fire on every horizon; rides for his life with a handful of men. Cut off, surrounded, rescued by Zhao Yun's relief; flees to Baidicheng with the war lost. The proud old emperor who had said 'I am a tiger' is broken in a single night.

Zhao Yun; the chapter's rescuer. Riding in from Chengdu hears of the fire; charges through Lu Xun's pursuit lines and cuts a path to Liu Bei; carries the emperor to Baidicheng. Kills the Wu pursuit officer Zhu Ran in single combat at the crossing.
Zhu Ran; in the Wu pursuit; killed by Zhao Yun.
Feng Xi; killed in the Yiling fire.
Feng Xi (duplicate record).
Zhang Nan; killed in the Yiling fire.
Zhang Nan (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao; escapes from the burning camps with Guan Xing.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Guan Xing; escapes the fire with Zhang Bao.
Ma Liang; rides on ahead to Chengdu with the camp-map; survives by being absent when the fire breaks.

Huang Quan; with the Shu north-bank army; cut off from Liu Bei's main force by the burnings; surrenders to Wei to save his men - Liu Bei refuses to harm his family, saying 'he did not betray me; I betrayed him.'
Cao Pi; receives Huang Quan and the news that Liu Bei has been crushed; orders three armies south against Wu while Sun Quan is exhausted.
Cao Ren; commands one of the three Wei armies invading Wu.
Cao Xiu; commands one of the three Wei armies.
Cao Zhen; commands one of the three Wei armies - the first major appearance of the future Wei chief commander.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Sun Zi; advises Cao Pi on the three-army offensive against Wu.
Ding Feng; with Lu Xun's army.
Ding Feng (duplicate record).
Han Dang; with Lu Xun's army.
Zhou Tai; with Lu Xun's army.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).
Xu Sheng; with Lu Xun's army; will distinguish himself defending Wu against Cao Pi's invasion.
Sun Huan; with Lu Xun's army.
Sun Huan (duplicate record).

Zhuge Liang; in Chengdu when Ma Liang arrives with the camp-map. Tears his beard at the layout - knows immediately Shu is doomed. The Eight Trigrams stone maze at Yufu Gorge was his earlier defensive measure, now traps Lu Xun and saves Liu Bei from total pursuit.
Du Lu; minor figure named in passing.
Lady Sun; throws herself into the river in despair at the chapter's wreckage.
Lady Sun (duplicate record).
Wu Qi; minor figure named in passing.
Liang Da; minor figure.
Liu Ning; named in passing.
Shentu Pan; minor figure.
Zhao Rong; minor figure.

Liu Bei dies at Baidicheng. The chapter's heart. From his sickbed at the Yong'an Palace (Baidicheng), summons Zhuge Liang from Chengdu. The famous 'orphan-entrusting' (托孤) scene: tells Zhuge Liang to support Liu Shan if he proves worthy - and to take the throne himself if he proves unworthy. Zhuge Liang weeps and prostrates himself swearing absolute loyalty. Dies aged 63; era Zhangwu year 3. Names Liu Shan emperor; appoints Zhuge Liang and Li Yan co-regents.

Zhuge Liang; receives the death-entrustment at Baidicheng. From this chapter forward he is effectively the head of state - Liu Shan's regent. Returns to Chengdu and reorganises the kingdom; defuses Cao Pi's five-army invasion through sleight of hand (covered in the chapter's second half).
Liu Shan; succeeds his father as Emperor of Shu-Han at sixteen. Era Jianxing year 1. Begins his long, mostly-Zhuge-Liang-managed reign.
Li Yan; named co-regent at Baidicheng alongside Zhuge Liang. The civil counterweight to Zhuge Liang's military authority - important later when he sabotages a Northern Expedition.
Li Yan (duplicate record).

Cao Pi; on learning Liu Bei has died, mobilises five armies against Shu - Sima Yi's plan. The Five-Route invasion: Cao Zhen against Yangping Pass; Meng Da from Shangyong; Sun Quan from Donghu Pass; the Qiang king from the west; Meng Huo from the south.
Sima Yi; designs the Five-Route plan against Shu; the cool strategist behind Cao Pi's offensive. Defused by Zhuge Liang routing each of the five separately without ever assembling them in battle.
Jia Xu; senior Wei counsel; dies of old age in this chapter.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).
Meng Huo; the Nanman king; bribed by Cao Pi to invade from the south. First appearance - the figure whom Zhuge Liang will famously capture seven times in chapters 87-90.
Meng Da; supposedly part of the five-army plan from Shangyong - Zhuge Liang sends Li Yan a personal letter that secretly nudges Meng Da into refusing to move. Defused.
Meng Da (duplicate record).
Sun Quan; supposedly part of the five-army plan from Donghu Pass - Deng Zhi is sent as Shu envoy and talks him back into the Shu-Wu alliance.

Deng Zhi; sent by Zhuge Liang as envoy to Sun Quan; revives the Shu-Wu alliance with a brilliant audience scene - confronted with a boiling cauldron meant to intimidate him, he calmly explains why Wu needs the alliance more than Shu does. The diplomatic comeback that saves Shu in its weakest moment.
Cao Zhen; commands the Yangping Pass army; Zhao Yun's defense holds him.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Zhao Yun; defends Yangping Pass against Cao Zhen's army; holds. The last of the Five Tigers still standing tall.
Wei Yan; with the Shu defense.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Ma Chao; defends the western frontier against the Qiang army; the Qiang remember him from his old days as 'Ma Chao the God of War'; turn back without fighting.
Ma Su; first major appearance as a senior advisor to Zhuge Liang - articulates the deflection of the five-army plan. The man Zhuge Liang will later execute at Jieting.
Ma Liang; named in passing - Ma Su's elder brother.
Cao Cao; named in passing - already dead.
Guan Yu; named in passing in Liu Bei's deathbed speech.
Zhang Fei; named in passing in Liu Bei's deathbed speech.
Zhang Bao; in Liu Bei's court.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Guan Xing; in Liu Bei's court.
Huang Quan; named in passing in the Liu Bei deathbed recap.
Liu Ye; on Cao Pi's council.
Lu Fan; on Sun Quan's council.
Lu Fan (duplicate record).
Lu Xun; on Sun Quan's council; advises against the Cao Pi alliance - Sun Quan agrees and re-aligns with Shu.
Zhu Huan; young Wu general; defeats Cao Ren's army when Cao Pi later invades - distinguishes himself.
Cao Ren; commands a Wei army against Wu; loses to Zhu Huan.
Cao Xiu; commands a Wei army against Wu.
Xiahou Shang; with the Wei armies; killed in the Wu campaign.
Zhang Liao; reappears with Cao Pi's Wu invasion - already aged and ill; killed by an arrow from Ding Feng. The last of the Five Wei generals (Cao Cao's named picks) gone.
Wang Shuang; minor Wei officer.
Wang Shuang (duplicate record).
Zhuge Jin; brokers the Wu side of the renewed alliance.
Zhuge Qian; minor Shu figure named in passing.
Lady Mi; named in passing - Liu Bei's old consort; long dead.
Empress Zhang; Liu Shan's empress (Zhang Fei's daughter); installed at his accession.
Empress Zhang (duplicate record).
Empress Zhang (duplicate record).
Dong Yun; in Liu Shan's new court.
Du Qiong; in Liu Shan's new court.
Chang Diao; minor figure named in passing.
Liu Li; in Liu Shan's court.
Shentu Pan; in Liu Shan's court.
Feng Xi; named in passing in the Yiling aftermath recap - killed at Yiling.
Feng Xi (duplicate record).
Zhang Nan; named in passing in the Yiling aftermath recap - killed at Yiling.
Zhang Nan (duplicate record).
